description: A drain or spoiled meal may 'pothu': give off an odour that a speaker or named experiencer finds strongly unpleasant. The judgement belongs to the odour in its situation; danger, cleanliness, and moral worth each need their own claim. 'whinu' (smell) is neutral perception, while 'mukesi' (spoil) judges a perishable thing unfit for its intended use.
articulatory_notes: Close the lips for unvoiced 'p' and release into stressed middle-height back rounded 'o'. Place the tongue tip lightly against the upper teeth for unvoiced 'th', pass air through the narrow gap, and finish with high-back rounded 'u'. Keep the dental fricative distinct from the complete closure of 't'.
sound_symbolism: A complete lip closure releases into rounded 'o', then sustained dental friction carries the second syllable into a tighter rounded vowel. The abrupt turn into rough airflow resembles the small recoil a strong odour may prompt.
usage_notes: 'pothu' is intransitive, with the source of the odour as its subject. When reactions differ, a separate 'whinu' (smell) or 'phaelo' (feel) clause can identify whose experience is being reported. Nominal 'pothu' is a stink or instance of stinking.

pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Odour is one clue in deciding whether stored food or water needs attention. 'pothu' keeps the strong sensory report available while suitability and cause remain separate questions.

semantic_domains.cognition: a contextual judgement that an odour is strongly unpleasant
semantic_domains.physical: a strong odour emitted from a source and received through the nose
